decision: The appliances are connected in parallel, giving each its own independent branch to the power supply so that switching one on or off does not affect the others.

positions.summary: In a parallel circuit, each appliance connects directly between the two supply conductors, receiving the same voltage independently. Switching one device on or off opens or closes only its own branch, leaving all other branches unaffected. Series circuits would cause all devices to go off when one is switched, and AC vs DC describes current type, not circuit isolation behavior.

The obvious answer is correct here. Parallel circuits give each appliance its own independent voltage path, so toggling one doesn't affect others. Series circuits would cause all appliances to fail when one is switched off. DC vs AC (A/D) describes current type, not circuit topology — irrelevant to independence. No credible alternative exists.

In a series circuit, turning off one appliance breaks the loop and cuts power to all others. In a parallel circuit, each appliance has its own independent branch to the power supply, so toggling one leaves the others unaffected. DC vs AC (A/D) describes current type, not independence of operation. Only parallel connection (C) explains why appliances operate independently.

subject:
Four significantly different household appliances (blender, mixer, toaster, and food processor) are plugged into four outlets along a counter. Turning on or off one or more of the appliances does not affect the performance of the other appliances because the four appliances

Options:
A. run on a direct current.
B. are connected in series.
C. are connected in parallel.
D. run on an alternating current.

Which option is correct?

decision: Earth and the Moon both have rocky crusts that include mountains. The other options are eliminated because the Moon has virtually no atmosphere (rules out A), the Moon's gravity is roughly 1/6 of Earth's (rules out B), and the Moon has no liquid water and therefore no water erosion features (rules out D).

positions.summary: Earth and the Moon both have rocky crusts with mountains. Earth's atmosphere is thick and oxygen-rich while the Moon has virtually none (eliminates A). Earth's gravity is ~6x stronger than the Moon's (eliminates B). Earth has extensive water erosion features, but the Moon has no liquid water and shows no water erosion (eliminates D). Rocky, mountainous crusts is the one geological feature genuinely shared by both bodies.

The obvious answer is C, and it holds up under scrutiny. Earth and the Moon both have rocky, differentiated crusts with mountain ranges (Earth's tectonic mountains; the Moon's highlands and impact-formed ranges). A is false—the Moon has virtually no atmosphere. B is false—Moon's gravity is ~1/6 Earth's. D is false—the Moon has no liquid water and shows no water erosion.

Eliminate A: Moon has virtually no atmosphere; Earth has a substantial one. Eliminate B: Earth's gravity is ~6× stronger than the Moon's. Eliminate D: Moon has no liquid water and shows no water erosion features. C survives: both Earth and Moon have rocky crusts with mountain ranges — Earth's Rockies/Himalayas, Moon's Apennine/Lunar Highlands — making this the shared feature.

subject:
Which of the following describes a feature 12 that is shared by Earth and the Moon?

Options:
A. They have nearly the same atmosphere.
B. They have almost the same gravitational pull.
C. They have a rocky crust that includes mountains.
D. They have areas that show considerable water erosion.

Which option is correct?

decision: The Sun's gravitational pull keeps Earth in orbit by continuously curving its path inward, counteracting Earth's tendency to travel in a straight line. The other options are either irrelevant (Moon's inertia, Earth's rotation) or describe effects rather than causes (orbital location).

positions.summary: Earth orbits the Sun because the Sun's gravitational pull continuously curves Earth's path inward. Without gravity, Earth would travel in a straight line (inertia). The Moon's inertia is irrelevant to Earth's orbit; orbital location is an effect, not a cause; and Earth's rotation on its axis has no bearing on its orbital path around the Sun.

The obvious answer is correct here. The Sun's gravitational pull is what keeps Earth in orbit — without it, Earth's inertia would carry it in a straight line into space. The moon's inertia (A) is irrelevant to Earth's solar orbit. 'Location of orbit' (B) describes a result, not a cause. Earth's rotation (C) is independent of its orbital mechanics. No compelling alternative exists.

Eliminating A: the Moon's inertia doesn't keep Earth in solar orbit. Eliminating B: orbit location is a consequence, not a cause. Eliminating C: Earth's rotation is independent of its orbital motion. D survives — the Sun's gravitational pull is the centripetal force that continuously bends Earth's path into an orbit, counterbalancing Earth's tangential velocity.

subject:
What keeps the Earth in orbit around the Sun?

Options:
A. inertia of the moon
B. location of earth's orbit
C. speed of Earth's rotation
D. Sun's gravitational pull

Which option is correct?
